subject: MTH
title: Fractions
objectives: Define a fraction as part of a whole
Distinguish proper fractions, improper fractions, and mixed numbers
Convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers

content.teaching_outline: Introduce a fraction as a part of a whole using a shared meat pie. One pie divided equally among 4 friends gives each person 1/4.
examples: A meat pie cut into 4 equal parts; one part is 1/4
A 60-minute lesson; 30 minutes is 1/2 of the lesson
misconceptions: Parts must be EQUAL; a pie cut unevenly does not give fair fractions

check_question.q: Ada cuts an orange into 8 equal pieces and eats 3. What fraction did she eat?

wrong_answer_hints.8/3: Careful! The pieces eaten go on top, the total parts go below.

visual_plan.live_animation_example: Draw circle, split into 8, shade 3, annotate 3/8

content.teaching_outline: Name the parts: top number (numerator) counts the parts we have; bottom number (denominator) counts the total equal parts.
examples: In 3/8, numerator is 3 and denominator is 8
misconceptions: A bigger denominator does not mean a bigger fraction; 1/8 is smaller than 1/4

check_question.q: In the fraction 5/12, which number is the denominator?

visual_plan.live_animation_example: Write 5/12 large, draw arrows labelling numerator and denominator

content.teaching_outline: Proper (numerator smaller, e.g. 2/5), improper (numerator equal or larger, e.g. 7/4), mixed number (whole plus fraction, e.g. 1 3/4). Connect improper and mixed with the same pie picture.
examples: 7/4 of a pie is the same as 1 whole pie and 3/4 of another
misconceptions: An improper fraction is not a mistake; it is a valid way to write more than one whole

check_question.q: Is 9/5 a proper fraction, an improper fraction, or a mixed number?

visual_plan.live_animation_example: Draw two circles, fill one fully and 3/4 of the second, show 7/4 = 1 3/4

segments.title: Converting Improper Fractions and Mixed Numbers

content.teaching_outline: Improper to mixed: divide numerator by denominator; quotient is the whole, remainder is the new numerator. Mixed to improper: whole times denominator plus numerator.
misconceptions: Students often forget to keep the SAME denominator after converting

visual_plan.live_animation_example: Step-by-step long division of 13 by 5 drawn live, then assemble 2 3/5

asset_library.description: A meat pie dividing into equal parts with one part highlighted, configurable parts count
A large fraction with numerator and denominator labelled with arrows
Three-column chart showing proper, improper, and mixed number examples with pie pictures
